channel.title: The Grant Desk - AI & Safety
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: AI & Safety.

item.title: GovAI Research Fellow (AI Governance) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: London, UK or Washington, DC preferred; open to candidates based elsewhere. UK visa sponsorship available; US sponsorship only in exceptional cases.

Award: Full-time 2-year fixed-term contract, renewable. Salary by experience and location: London typically GBP 85,000-110,000; Washington DC typically USD 110,000-180,000, with additional compensation for higher cost-of-living regions such as the SF Bay Area. Benefits: health, dental and vision insurance; GBP 5,000 (~USD 6,000) annual wellbeing budget; GBP 5,000 (~USD 6,000) annual professional-development budget; ~USD 500/month coworking for remote staff; flexible hours; extended parental leave; 10% employer pension contribution; 33 days paid vacation (including public holidays). No application fee.

Duration: 2-year full-time contract, renewable

Deadline: 17 Aug 2026

Research Fellows play a central role in GovAI's research: experienced researchers who conduct independent, high-quality work with direct relevance to AI governance and policy while contributing to GovAI's intellectual community. NOTE: applications open at the end of July 2026; GovAI does not publish a fixed closing date, so the 17 August 2026 date shown here is just when this listing is set to expire (check the GovAI page for the live status). Work may take the form of academic publications, policy memos, reports or blog posts, alongside mentoring early-career researchers (such as Summer and Winter Fellows), shaping GovAI's research strategy and convening stakeholders. Fellows work under a Senior Research Fellow or leadership-team member but have substantial autonomy over projects, agendas and publication formats. TOPICS include risk management, threat modeling, economics of AI, geopolitics of AI, public policy and technical governance; candidates working on adjacent topics are also welcome. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of academic and professional backgrounds (computer science, political science, public policy, economics, philosophy, law, plus government/industry/civil-society experience); no strict educational requirement, but the strongest candidates typically have multiple years of relevant research or policy experience and a track record of high-quality, impactful work. A shared application also covers a one-year visiting Research Scholar position for those with less experience (apply once for both). PROCESS: written application; a paid remote work test (some workstreams add a research agenda and a second paid test); then a final stage of two interviews and reference checks. Deep expertise in technical governance, frontier safety design, cybersecurity or US policy is especially welcome. Contact recruitment@governance.ai with location or access questions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: IAS Civic Science Fellow: AI and Society (Nelson Lab) 2026-27
item.description:
Organisation: Institute for Advanced Study (IAS), Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab

Location: Princeton, NJ, USA (in-person)

Award: USD 7,277.77 per month (about USD 87,300/year) for the 18-month full-time appointment, plus a comprehensive benefits package (medical, dental and vision insurance; life and short/long-term disability coverage; flexible spending accounts; paid holidays; retirement plans; tuition assistance; employee assistance program). No application fee.

Duration: 18-month full-time fellowship (2026-27)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Institute for Advanced Study is seeking a 2026-27 Civic Science Fellow to advance understanding of the social, democratic and scientific implications of artificial intelligence. The full-time, 18-month position is based in the Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab led by Harold F. Linder Professor of Social Science Alondra Nelson. Working closely with Professor Nelson and Lab members, the Fellow contributes to research and policy analysis and to scholarly and public-facing publications, and supports multidisciplinary, multisector initiatives at the intersection of AI, science and governance. DUTIES (75% Lab work): conduct original research on the Lab's AI-and-society agenda; synthesize scholarship on the social implications of AI, sociotechnical approaches to AI governance and the epistemological implications of AI in research; contribute policy research on governance mechanisms that strengthen democratic institutions; help organize convenings and working groups; collaborate on manuscripts, reports and grant proposals; support editorial development of publications; and develop public-facing essays, policy briefs and digital materials. The remaining 25% supports the Fellow's own independent research, network-building and full participation in the intellectual life of IAS. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: a PhD in the social sciences, sciences or humanities; a strong record of scholarly research, writing and publication on the social implications of science and technology or related fields; and exceptional writing and editing skills for both academic and non-academic audiences. Preferred: specialization in science and technology studies, science and technology policy, organizational studies or qualitative methods, and demonstrated engagement with AI governance and technology policy debates. Apply with a cover letter and resume via the IAS/Paylocity posting.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Manifund: AI Safety Regranting (Rolling)
item.description:
Organisation: Manifund

Location: Remote; US-based platform, open worldwide.

Award: Grants typically USD 5,000 to 50,000, recommended by independent regrantors from their own budgets. No application fee. No legal entity required.

Duration: Varies by project (rolling)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Manifund's regranting program funds AI safety and global-catastrophic-risk work through a network of expert regrantors, each given an independent budget to recommend grants to people and projects they find promising. Applicants submit a project proposal on the Manifund platform; regrantors can then offer funding. GRANTS: typically USD 5,000 to 50,000, though amounts vary. ELIGIBILITY: individuals (no legal entity or institutional affiliation required) and organizations working on AI safety or reducing catastrophic risk from AI. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis; per-regrantor budget availability varies over time. Learn more at the regranting page and apply on the Manifund platform (manifund.org).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Paid Research Assistant: AI and the War in Ukraine
item.description:
Organisation: Bogna Konior (NYU Shanghai)

Location: Remote-friendly.

Award: USD 18 per hour; 10-15 hours per week, flexible schedule.

Duration: Ongoing part-time research assistantship; start as soon as possible.

Deadline: Closed 23 Jul 2026

Bogna Konior, Assistant Professor of Media Theory at NYU Shanghai, is hiring a paid research assistant for a project on Ukraine and artificial intelligence. The work supports a forthcoming book with MIT Press, plus an independent scenario she is writing on the future of AI with the war as a lens. WHAT THE WORK LOOKS LIKE: going month-by-month through news archives (New York Times, Reuters, Financial Times, and specialist outlets), annotating and cataloguing coverage of AI, drones, and autonomous systems in the war, plus other developments she will direct you to; building and maintaining a structured Notion database covering chronology, companies, industries, and key actors; and organizing and editing her existing notes. WHO SHE IS LOOKING FOR: interest in and general knowledge of AI; prior research and annotation experience, careful and detail-oriented, no sloppy citations; comfortable with Notion and with AI tools; Ukrainian language skill a strong plus (not required); familiarity with AI, war, tech, and geopolitics news sources is great. Remote-friendly, start ASAP. She will also consider people with no prior experience, as well as current and previous students. TERMS: USD 18 per hour, 10-15 hours per week, flexible schedule. TO APPLY: email bogna@nyu.edu with a short introduction and background (link to website, socials, CV, LinkedIn, or portfolio if you have one); where you are based and your weekly availability; and one recent AI-related story, company, or development you find interesting and why (a vibe check). Bonus: a sample of past research or writing if you have it.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AGI Governance Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Johns Hopkins University, School of Government and Policy (SOGP)

Location: Hopkins Bloomberg Center, Washington, D.C. (in-person only)

Award: Fully funded: travel, accommodation, and meals covered.

Duration: Three-week in-person intensive, 8-25 September 2026

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The AGI Governance Fellowship is the inaugural three-week intensive run by Johns Hopkins University's School of Government and Policy (SOGP) to develop the next generation of leaders in AI governance. COVERS: frontier AI legislation at state and federal levels; building societal resilience amid rapid capability growth; democratic practices in AGI alignment; and designing new governing institutions for the AGI era. WHO SHOULD APPLY: early-career researchers and practitioners (within roughly eight years of undergrad) with solid foundations in AI governance, including congressional staffers, AGI company employees, recent CS or policy PhDs, and nonprofit/civil society professionals; a postgraduate degree or equivalent experience is required. This is a capstone program for rising leaders, not an intro course. FUNDING: travel, accommodation, and meals are fully covered. DATES: 8-25 September 2026 at the Hopkins Bloomberg Center in Washington, D.C. (in-person only). DEADLINE: apply by 15 July 2026 for full consideration; rolling applications accepted. APPLY: https://sogp.jh.edu/agi-governance-fellowship/

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Horizon Institute for Public Service: AI Rapid Response Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service

Location: Washington, DC area, United States (in-person; full-time placements in U.S. executive branch offices).

Award: Fellow track: USD 170,000 to 200,000+, plus an additional stipend for benefits. Senior Fellow track: from USD 250,000+, plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement.

Duration: One year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal.

Deadline: Closed 22 Jul 2026

The AI Rapid Response Fellowship is a focused effort by the Horizon Institute for Public Service to close the AI security talent gap in the U.S. federal government. Horizon is recruiting a specialized cohort of exceptional technical and policy talent to work full-time in executive branch offices on AI security-related issues, with placements beginning as soon as possible. TRACKS: the Fellow track is for candidates with technical or policy expertise relevant to AI security, typically 2-6+ years of full-time relevant experience; the Senior Fellow track is for candidates with substantial prior experience, typically 10-15+ years in relevant technical, policy, or leadership roles. FOCUS: AI governance, AI security, policy, and public service. COMPENSATION: Fellow track USD 170,000-200,000+ plus a benefits stipend; Senior Fellow track from USD 250,000+ plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement. DURATION: one year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal, based in the Washington, DC area. ELIGIBILITY: U.S. citizenship is required. An existing security clearance, or eligibility to obtain one, is helpful but not required. DEADLINE: applications are due July 22, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Google Data Center Community AI Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Google (with Watson Institute)

Location: Google Data Center communities, United States. Fellowship delivered with mentorship and training.

Award: Fully funded fellowship including AI training, mentorship, Google resources, and a USD 1,000 community development stipend. No application fee.

Duration: Fellowship program with AI training and mentorship (dates per program).

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

The Google Data Center Community AI Fellowship is a fully funded fellowship for community leaders and entrepreneurs using AI or technology to create local impact in Google Data Center communities. Fellows receive AI training, mentorship, access to Google resources, and a USD 1,000 community development stipend to support projects that deliver local impact. WHO IT'S FOR: community leaders and entrepreneurs applying AI or technology to community-level challenges in Google Data Center communities. WHAT'S OFFERED: AI training, mentorship, Google resources, and a USD 1,000 stipend. PRIORITY DEADLINE: 12 July 2026. TO APPLY: submit the application via the Watson Institute program page.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AIxDESIGN x KB: Open Call for Sessions, (Art)works and Performances
item.description:
Organisation: AIxDESIGN, co-presented with the Koninklijke Bibliotheek (KB), National Library of the Netherlands

Location: KB, The Hague, Netherlands (in-person exhibition and programme); pitches welcome from people based anywhere

Award: Fees by track: opening-night experience EUR 1,500 to 2,000; a session in the events programme EUR 2,000 to 2,500; an existing (art)work for the exhibition EUR 200 to 400 honorarium (including licensing and production). The fee stays the same wherever you are based.

Duration: Exhibition runs November 2026 to February 2027; opening event November 2026

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

AIxDESIGN and the KB (Koninklijke Bibliotheek, the National Library of the Netherlands) are building an exhibition and public programme about AI, made from the national collection, at the KB in The Hague. Nadia Piet is Creative Lead with the AIxDESIGN community, co-presented with the KB. The premise: long before neural nets, libraries were the machines we built to organise human knowledge, and the show uses the KB's collection to look at what AI is, where it comes from, and how it connects to the older story of how we store, sort, and search what we know. Part of the show lives in the KB Atelier, with talks, workshops, and screenings alongside, intended as AI literacy you can wander into. WHAT THEY WANT: work that engages AI in some way (critical, creative, hands-on, or playful); bonus if it also speaks to archives, libraries, or how we store and sort knowledge, though you do not need to touch the KB's collection to take part. You may pitch to more than one track: (A) SOMETHING FOR THE OPENING NIGHT, a performance, talk, screening, tour, ritual, booth, game, or other experience for the November 2026 opening, fee EUR 1,500 to 2,000; (B) A SESSION IN THE EVENTS PROGRAMME, a workshop or other session once during the three-month run, around 2.5 to 4 hours, fee EUR 2,000 to 2,500; (C) AN (ART)WORK FOR THE EXHIBITION, existing work in any format (installation, sculpture, zine stand, a corner of your own) sturdy enough for a public area with daily traffic, honorarium EUR 200 to 400 including licensing and production; (D) SOMETHING ELSE ENTIRELY, pitch it anyway. WHO: artists, designers, researchers, technologists, performers, musicians, archivists, or educators working around AI, data, libraries, or cultural heritage; no finished idea or polished CV needed, just a clear, specific pitch. You can be based anywhere and they especially welcome people outside the Netherlands; the show is in-person in The Hague and the fee stays the same wherever you are coming from. HOW TO APPLY: send a short pitch through the form (which track, your idea in a paragraph or two, roughly what it would need, and a link to your work); takes about five minutes, no full application required. Deadline: end of August 2026. Questions to hello@aixdesign.co.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Singapore AI Safety Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Singapore AI Safety Hub (SASH)

Location: Singapore (three-month in-person fellowship, 21 September - 4 December 2026)

Award: Stipend of SGD 5,000 per month, plus housing and travel. Up to USD 30,000 in compute per project. Includes mentorship, research management support, and central office space in Chinatown.

Duration: Three months in Singapore, 21 September to 4 December 2026

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

The Singapore AI Safety Fellowship is an independent research fellowship that connects fellows with leading mentors working on technical AI safety and governance. Fellows conduct research for three months in Singapore (21 September to 4 December 2026), building the skills and relationships for international coordination on AI safety. It is built for researchers who want their work to inform real-world AI safety practice on top of publishing it, drawing on Singapore's role as a globally connected, policy-engaged hub. The program rests on three pillars: cross-regional collaboration (bringing together researchers from East and West to build trusted relationships for sustained cross-border AI safety collaboration); technical depth and research excellence (guided by experienced researchers and advisors, fellows investigate pressing challenges in making frontier AI systems safe, secure, and trustworthy); and applying research to policy (institutional partnerships that ground projects in real-world application and help fellows translate technical ideas into practical frameworks and policy recommendations). Advisors include Zhang Ya-Qin (Chair Professor, Tsinghua University; Founding Dean of the Institute for AI Industry Research) and Ryan Kidd (Co-Executive Director, MATS Program). Fellows receive weekly mentorship from established AI safety researchers, dedicated Research Managers, a stipend of SGD 5,000 per month with housing provided throughout, up to USD 30,000 in compute for projects that require it, central office space at SASH's hub in Chinatown, and a community of talks, workshops, and social events. The application deadline is 10 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AIxDESIGN: Grain, Open Call for Critical AI Literacy Recipes
item.description:
Organisation: AIxDESIGN, co-produced with the Netherlands Film Academy

Location: Remote/online; open to educators and practitioners based anywhere

Award: Need-based fee of EUR 150 per recipe for freelance and independent practitioners (email hello@aixdesign.co if the time would be hard to make without it). Your recipe is formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, plus a print copy and an invite to the launch. Published under CC BY-SA 4.0; you keep authorship.

Duration: One-off contribution; Grain publishes March 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

Grain is a cookbook of critical and creative AI literacy exercises for educators across audiovisual and creative practice, co-produced by AIxDESIGN and the Netherlands Film Academy and led by Nadia Piet. The open call collects 'recipes': tried-and-tested group exercises for the classroom or workshop that get people thinking critically and making creatively with AI. Exercises can be hands-on or analog, 15 minutes or a full day, beginner or advanced; the one hard requirement is that someone else can read your recipe and run it themselves. Examples of what might fit: hands-on experiments with specific AI tools (image generators, chatbots, code assistants, video models); analog activities that reveal computational logic through embodied practice; critical readings, watchings, or listenings with structured discussion; provocations, games, or role-plays that get at how AI sees, thinks, or makes; and exercises that help people figure out how they want to relate to AI in their own practice. WHAT YOU GET: your recipe formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, a print copy when it lands, and an invite to the launch. Everything is published under CC BY-SA 4.0 so the work can travel and you keep authorship. FEE: this round offers a need-based fee for freelance and independent practitioners of EUR 150 per recipe; if making the time would be hard without it, email hello@aixdesign.co with your proposal. After this round the call stays open but the fee will not. LIVE SESSION: an optional 30-minute live session on Wednesday 12 August 2026 at 16:00 CEST lets you bring a rough exercise and turn it into a recipe on the spot with the Grain Recipe Converter (online, sign up at luma.com/84w148in). Submissions are open until the end of August 2026; Grain publishes March 2027. Submit via the form; questions to hello@aixdesign.co.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)

Location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).

Award: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.

Duration: 9-12 months, starting fall 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Digital Democracy Centre x TrygFonden Fellowship Program 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Digital Democracy Centre (University of Southern Denmark), with support from TrygFonden

Location: Odense, Denmark (University of Southern Denmark). Flexible mix of in-office stays at the DDC in Odense and remote periods; stays planned to the Danish working calendar.

Award: Either a salary subsidy or a stipend to help with travel and/or housing; specific benefits (financial support, access to DDC research resources, flexible working spaces, speaker series, networks) vary by agreement between the fellow, DDC, and partnering organizations. Applicants describe the posts they seek support for (flights, housing, research costs, salary compensation, stipend, etc.).

Duration: Flexible; typically 2-6 months (minimum two months, can include shorter DDC stays and remote periods), longer in exceptional cases. Fellowships start anywhere in 2027.

Deadline: 01 Sep 2026

The Digital Democracy Centre (DDC) at the University of Southern Denmark, with support from TrygFonden, is calling for applications for its 2027 fellowship cohort. The program fosters collaboration between researchers, journalists, and policymakers exploring the impact of digital technology and AI on media, politics, and democracy. Fellows collaborate with a self-selected DDC mentor to generate new knowledge, contribute to public debate, develop networks, and produce tangible outreach outputs such as policy briefs, educational packages, and talks. THREE FELLOWSHIP TYPES: (1) academic fellows researching digital technologies and democracy; (2) journalist fellows covering digital technology and democracy; (3) policy fellows contributing to the regulatory debate. Target group: researchers, journalism graduates, and policymakers in research/educational institutions, media organizations, ministries, NGOs, etc. PRIORITIES: projects with clear relevance to Denmark or Danish society (including comparative/international projects yielding insights relevant to a Danish context); in 2027, projects related to DDC's Hybrid Warfare Initiative (societal resilience to new forms of international conflict, with a focus on digital technologies and AI) are also prioritized. EXPECTATIONS: applicants must secure confirmation of mentorship from their desired DDC mentor BEFORE applying (applications without a confirmed mentor are automatically rejected; PhD students and research assistants are generally not eligible as mentors, and Sten Schaumburg-Muller is unavailable as a mentor for the 2027 call); applicants must also coordinate support from their current supervisors/managers. Fellows engage in DDC activities, seminars/workshops, and public outreach, and submit a short report within two months of finishing. APPLICATION (in English): preliminary project title, fellowship type, brief project description (max 1000 chars), detailed description and timeline (max 2400 chars, including duration, confirmed mentor, and remote/in-office work plan), description of activities and collaboration with external practice partners (max 1000 chars), expected product/impact/output (max 500 chars), a CV (max 5 pages), and an overview of the posts for which financial support is sought. Submit the application form (PDF or Word) plus CV as PDFs to ddc@sdu.dk with subject line 'DDC / TrygFonden Fellowship application'. Deadline 1 September 2026; decisions communicated no later than 1 October 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (hybrid; remote participation available)

Award: Free to participate; in-person attendees provided dinner (value kept at or below USD 20 per session)

Duration: 12-week part-time program, early September to early December 2026 (approximately 5 hours per week)

Deadline: Closed 05 Jul 2026

The GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program is a bipartisan, part-time, 12-week program designed to accelerate or launch careers in U.S. AI policy. Open to U.S. policy professionals across government and think tanks, it builds a technically-informed understanding of the AI policy landscape through guided discussion sessions, with an emphasis on general-purpose AI. Topics include how general-purpose AI systems are developed; inputs to AI development such as energy, chips, and data centers; AI's effects on productivity, scientific discovery, and economic growth; national security and great power competition; corporate self-governance; AI regulation in other jurisdictions; and federal and state AI regulatory proposals in the U.S. Participants also have weekly opportunities to question world-leading experts; prior guest speakers have included White House OSTP staffers, former frontier-lab employees, and people who helped draft U.S. state- and federal-level AI policy proposals. The program runs early September to early December 2026 (break for Thanksgiving week) with a roughly 5-hour weekly commitment: two hours of synchronous evening sessions (EST), held in-person at GovAI's D.C. office near DuPont Circle or remotely with cameras on, plus three hours of independent reading and podcasts from a free syllabus. To list completion on a resume, participants must attend 9 of 12 sessions, write a short policy memo or op-ed, and pass an interview-based exam. Eligible participants include executive and legislative branch staff (fellows, interns, detailees, military personnel, national laboratory personnel) and think-tank policy researchers and affiliates, across a range of seniority levels, with preference for the D.C. metropolitan area; participants need not currently cover AI but should aspire to a career in U.S. tech or national security policy. Selection has two stages: an online application with six biographical questions, a resume, two verbal reasoning questions, and an approximately 7-minute video; followed by a 30-minute interview in July, with final decisions in early August.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: AI and Philanthropy
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: Closed 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for AI and Philanthropy, an internally-facing role focused on cross-programmatic learning and advisory support. Reporting to the Director, Technology and Society, the AI Tech Fellow will support Foundation staff in building and adapting internal frameworks for evaluating AI use in ways that prioritize mission and strategy and address ethical, sociotechnical, and operational considerations. Responsibilities include designing and curating hands-on trainings, workshops, and learning sessions to help staff build practical familiarity with AI tools and critically assess their value and efficacy; creating accessible documentation and guides on mission-aligned AI experimentation; curating sociotechnical conversations on how philanthropy and the nonprofit sector are experimenting with AI in ways that respect privacy, safety, and equity; monitoring emerging global AI developments and providing timely briefings to program teams; conducting landscape research on AI initiatives in the philanthropy and social justice sectors; and building relationships with AI researchers, practitioners, grantees and partners working on mission-enabling AI. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years working within public-interest, government, civil society, or commercial technology sectors; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; demonstrated sociotechnical expertise in artificial intelligence and related technologies including machine learning systems and generative AI and their societal implications; and experience designing, implementing or supporting AI applications and tools in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office). Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: OpenAI Economic Research Exchange 2026
item.description:
Organisation: OpenAI

Location: Remote / global. Structured, project-based research collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research. No location restriction stated.

Award: USD 25,000 project grant plus a USD 7,500 monthly stipend per selected researcher.

Duration: Carefully scoped projects with defined milestones. OpenAI supports a portfolio across time horizons: short-term projects (2-6 months) using existing approved data, and medium-term studies (6-12 months) that track outcomes over time or test specific interventions.

Deadline: Closed 05 Jul 2026

The OpenAI Economic Research Exchange is a new program supporting high-impact external research on the economic effects of AI. Through the Exchange, selected researchers conduct work via structured, project-based collaborations with OpenAI Economic Research, with the goal of producing credible, independent evidence on how AI is affecting workers, firms, institutions, and the broader economy. OpenAI is seeking projects that address important questions about the economic impacts of AI; proposals should explain how carefully governed, privacy-protected use of OpenAI tools and datasets can help answer these questions, extending beyond what traditional datasets alone allow, while maintaining clear safeguards for user privacy and responsible data use. Core questions include labor market effects (moving beyond AI exposure to measure which occupations are likely to see augmentation, displacement, new task creation, or employment growth as model capabilities improve) and measuring economic value (willingness to pay, revealed value, task-level time savings, avoided costs, quality-adjusted output, and durable productivity gains). Selected researchers undertake carefully scoped projects with defined milestones, data governance, and review processes. WHO SHOULD APPLY: researchers with strong empirical skills and relevant expertise in applied causal inference, measurement, labor economics, productivity, firms, education, entrepreneurship, public finance, regional economics, development, inequality, or related fields. EVALUATION: proposals are assessed on methodological rigor, feasibility, fit with Exchange priorities, clear milestones, and the potential to contribute credible external evidence on AI's economic impacts. The Exchange builds on OpenAI's broader measurement efforts, including OpenAI Signals. For more information see the Request for Proposals, or contact econresearch@openai.com. Applications close 5 July 2026; selected researchers will be notified by 31 July 2026.

item.title: AI Impact Scaling Program 2026 (Tech To The Rescue)
item.description:
Organisation: Tech To The Rescue

Location: NGOs and social impact organizations from around the world. Requires a project lead able to commit at least 5 hours per week over the 12-month program.

Award: No-cost program. Participating organizations are estimated to receive between USD 150,000 and USD 330,000 worth of services at no cost, including a matched pro bono technology partner, scaling strategy support, a tailored AI roadmap, responsible-AI/cybersecurity/data-governance guidance, mentors, infrastructure credits, technical resources, and visibility with funders and investors via a global Demo Day.

Duration: 12-month program.

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

The AI Impact Scaling Program from Tech To The Rescue helps nonprofits and social impact organizations use AI to expand the reach and effectiveness of interventions that already demonstrate measurable results. The focus is scaling proven impact, not proving it. Selected organizations get access to an ecosystem designed to turn proven programs into scalable AI-powered solutions, including: a matched pro bono technology partner from a network of 2,000+ software companies and AI experts; scaling strategy support and a tailored AI roadmap; specialized guidance on responsible AI, cybersecurity, and data governance; access to mentors, infrastructure credits, and technical resources; and visibility with funders and investors through a global Demo Day. The program estimates participating organizations receive between USD 150,000 and USD 330,000 worth of services at no cost. WHO SHOULD APPLY: organizations with a proven intervention, real users, measurable outcomes, and clear potential to scale; NGOs and social impact organizations from around the world; teams able to dedicate a project lead for at least five hours per week over the 12-month program. You do not need to have an AI solution already built; the program helps identify where AI can accelerate impact and then build the solution alongside experienced technology partners. Application deadline 30 June 2026.

Horizon Europe Cluster 2 funds research on culture, creativity, and inclusive society. The 2026 call (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE, launched May 12, 2026) covers 7 topics with an indicative budget of €85.5 million. Topic HERITAGE-04 is explicitly titled 'Towards a fair and transparent market for cultural and creative content in the era of generative AI,' covering: impacts of generative AI on creative industries employment; upskilling needs for creative workers; policy and regulatory frameworks; fair remuneration and transparency. item.title: AI Research Fellow / Senior AI Policy Researcher (Utah Office of AI Policy)
item.description:
Organisation: Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy

Location: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States. Hybrid telework may be available but regular in-person work is required, and in-state work rules require all work to be performed while physically within Utah. No visa sponsorship.

Award: Salary: USD 33.13 to 54.01 hourly wage. Full-time, time-limited position. Job classification Research Consultant III (#16048); may underfill as Research Consultant II (#16058) with a reduced salary range.

Duration: Full-time, time-limited (Schedule Code TL).

Deadline: Closed 24 Jun 2026

The Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy, is seeking an AI Research Fellow to help drive technical, scientific, and policy research for Utah's AI Learning Lab. This is an applied research and policy-translation role whose work should directly inform regulatory decisions, legislative recommendations, regulatory mitigation agreements, and stakeholder strategy. The role is technical and relies heavily on statistical, machine learning, computer science, and empirical research skills, while also requiring strong judgment, clear writing, and the ability to operate in a small multidisciplinary team alongside colleagues with policy, business, regulatory, and legal expertise. The Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy was created in May 2024 to systematically develop sensible AI policy across major impact areas such as healthcare, education, finance, transportation, and consumer technology. Central to the Office is the AI Learning Lab, where industry experts, academics, regulators, and policymakers work toward appropriate regulatory responses to emerging AI applications; the Office also has authority to create regulatory mitigation agreements that let responsible AI businesses test products in Utah under structured conditions while the state gathers evidence. KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: independently scope and execute research projects on emerging AI policy questions (defining the question, identifying technical and empirical evidence, interviewing experts, evaluating tradeoffs, and producing written recommendations); research the technical aspects, capabilities, limitations, and societal impacts of AI technologies; evaluate evidence and forecasts on the impact of human-AI interactions on health, economic activity, education, consumer welfare, and public institutions; draft executive policy memos, legislative briefings, technical explainers, risk assessments, and regulatory mitigation analyses; interface with business leaders, technologists, researchers, regulators, legislators, and lawyers; and advise on the development of regulatory and legal standards. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: deep technical knowledge of statistical methods, machine learning, and AI systems; strong ability to translate technical and empirical evidence into practical policy options for nontechnical decision-makers; familiarity with human-subject research, qualitative and quantitative methods, medical or health sciences, social sciences, human-computer interaction, or related empirical fields; excellent problem-solving, reliability, and independent judgment; and strong technical communication across policy, legal, regulatory, business, and technical audiences. PREFERRED: experience implementing, evaluating, or deploying AI systems (business, healthcare, government, or other applied settings); expertise in public policy, law, regulatory design, or government decision-making; ability to evaluate technical claims, research methods, and evidence quality; specific healthcare experience; and experience supporting AI deployment in regulated environments. A criminal history check is required. Recruiter: Tehra Gorski, tgorski@utah.gov, 801-530-6030. Application deadline 24 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. item.title: microagi Research Fellowship - Cohort 01
item.description:
Organisation: microagi

Location: Munich, Germany or Zurich, Switzerland; in-person, 6-month residence.

Award: Up to $2,000,000 in compute resources; $10,000/month stipend. No application fee stated. $10,000 referral bonus if you refer an accepted fellow.

Duration: 6 months in residence.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The microagi Research Fellowship (Cohort 01) provides researchers with frontier-lab resources and the freedom to work on hard problems in robotics, embodied AI, and the systems around them. Fellows work in residence for six months at microagi's offices in Munich or Zurich. AMOUNT: up to $2,000,000 in compute; $10,000/month stipend. RESEARCH AREAS: (1) Long Horizon Dexterous Manipulation -- end-to-end models for complex multi-stage tasks with dexterous hands, natural language instruction following, memory across long task sequences, fine-grained physical control in unstructured environments; (2) Cross Embodiment Learning; (3) On Device Physical Intelligence; (4) Safe and Reliable Deployment. microagi's model is to deploy embodied intelligence into physical-world operations and turn what it learns there into compounding research. REFERRAL: $10,000 bonus for referring a candidate who is accepted into the cohort. No application deadline publicly stated as of June 2026 -- one cohort only, apply as soon as possible via https://www.microagi.ai/fellowship.

Open call details

item.title: Center for Humane Technology: Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship (Inaugural Cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Humane Technology (CHT)

Location: Remote, 10 hours/week overlapping with EST required.

Award: $30,000 fixed stipend paid in three milestone-based tranches. No application fee.

Duration: 6 months: September 14, 2026 through March 18, 2027. 15 hours per week.

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

CHT's inaugural Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship places 3 fellows (one per focus area) in a 6-month, part-time program to develop rigorous public-impact research and writing on how AI is reshaping society. Fellows choose from 5 focus areas: (1) AI and Cognition — what AI does to human judgment, critical thinking, and intellectual independence; (2) AI and Relationships — AI's effects on intimacy, companionship, and social bonds; (3) AI and Surveillance — how AI enables monitoring, tracking, and control; (4) AI and Spirituality — AI's intersection with meaning, transcendence, and religious practice; (5) Inside the Machine: AI From a Technologist's Perspective. Up to 2 focus areas per application. DELIVERABLES: one flagship research piece, report, or long-form essay for public impact; 1-3 op-eds (placed in target outlets or CHT Substack); one podcast appearance (Your Undivided Attention or equivalent); cohort meetings and bi-weekly check-ins. SUPPORT: bi-weekly guidance from Senior Director of Strategy and Impact; editorial, communications and graphic design support; media training and PR placement support; CHT email address and Slack access; listing on CHT's public fellows page. WHO SHOULD APPLY: mid-career researchers (psychology, sociology, philosophy, cognitive science, communications, STS), policy analysts, journalists covering tech and society, civil society advocates, or technologists with insider perspective. Strong preference for those with a public writing track record (op-eds, Substack, peer-reviewed work) and some public presence. HOW TO APPLY: 1-2 page research proposal per topic (up to 2), CV/resume (2 pages max), 2-minute video explaining fit, writing sample. Deadline: Sunday 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

Open call details

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Research Fellowships 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote or institutional (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: $55,000–$120,000/year through a university, or $80,000–$150,000/year direct stipend; plus research budget (10% of salary), conference travel, and compute resources where applicable. No application fee.

Duration: 1–2 years. Second year conditional on continuing to meet programme requirements.

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy Research Fellowships support scholars pursuing research on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, and legal position of AI systems. AMOUNT: $55,000–$120,000/year (through a university, with 10% research budget and standard fringe benefits), or $80,000–$150,000/year (direct stipend, with suggested 5–10% allocation for research and travel). Compute resources available for machine learning research. Invitation and funding to attend the annual Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy Summit at NYU. ELIGIBILITY: must have or be expected to receive a doctoral degree in computer science, neuroscience, a related technical discipline, law, medicine, or applied social science by September 2027 or fellowship start date (exceptions for equivalent research experience); fellows arrange their own institutional affiliation; fellowship may be conditional on institutional affiliation or attendance at events. SELECTION CRITERIA: demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relation to AI risk; evidence of research promise (publications, academic achievements, references). DURATION: 1–2 years; second year conditional on ongoing programme requirements. AREAS OF INTEREST: introspection, welfare measurement, interpretability, model character, correlates of consciousness, valence research, governance frameworks, legal and political responses to potentially sentient AI. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Required application materials: CV, description of possible research directions (~1,000 words), intended outcomes, what you would do without this fellowship (~150–400 words), career goals (~300 words). Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

item.title: Híbrides: Small Embodied Data - Call for Contributions (2nd Edition)
item.description:
Organisation: Axolot.cat collective and DARTS research group, Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC)

Location: Barcelona, Spain; UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí. In-person attendance required.

Award: Oral presentations: €100 honorarium (invoice required). Performances: €400 honorarium (invoice required). No submission fee.

Duration: Festival runs 26-28 November 2026.

Deadline: 07 Sep 2026

Híbrides is a biennial of artistic research co-organised by the Axolot.cat collective and the DARTS (Design, Art, Technology and Society) research group at the UOC in Barcelona. The second edition, "Small Embodied Data," centres on the concept of classification -- approached through machine learning, data science, algorithmic systems, and questions of who designs classifiers, under what ethical codes, and to what end. The biennial brings classification into dialogue with its previous themes of Small Data (community-defined, intentional data collection and analysis) and Embodiment (bodily experience as a foundation of situated, sensory knowledge). Both academic and non-academic contributions are welcome. FORMATS ACCEPTED: Oral Presentations (20 minutes; artistic practice, artistic research, or academic research projects; may be video-recorded for a public archive and developed into short papers); Performances (20-30 minutes; must engage with the physical body, stage, and technology; complete works, including previously presented work; takes place in a multipurpose space with technical support). SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS: online form with abstract (max 1,000 characters), professional biography (max 1,000 characters), and link to representative materials (3-5 images; video and technical rider required for performances). HONORARIA: oral presentations €100; performances €400; invoice required in both cases. Participants must follow the event's code of conduct. Event runs 26-28 November 2026 at UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí, Barcelona. Deadline: 7 September 2026. Submit at https://hibrides.axolot.cat/opencall.html.

Open call details

item.title: GovAI DC Winter Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, DC, USA; in-person, full-time (part-time possible for DC-based think tank or government staff).

Award: $21,000 stipend for the three-month term, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch provided. No visa sponsorship. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

GovAI's DC Winter Fellowship is a three-month bipartisan in-person fellowship in Washington, DC, designed for individuals seeking to enter or advance in US AI governance and policy. The program emphasises bipartisan engagement, rigorous analysis, and practical policy relevance, with a focus on how AI policy is shaped within the US political system. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice with mentorship from a GovAI supervisor; the first two weeks explore the AI policy landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI policy. Fellows also attend expert Q&As, skills workshops, seminars, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US government bodies (NSC, OSTP, Senate, Department of Commerce, DHS); think tanks (CSIS, CSET, CNAS, RAND, Heritage Foundation, FAI); AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind); and universities (Yale, Stanford, MIT). AMOUNT: $21,000 stipend for three months, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch. Part-time fellowship at a pro-rated salary available for DC-based think tank researchers, government staffers, or professionals doing AI governance work; pay accommodations possible. ELIGIBILITY: candidates must have the right to work in the US; no visa sponsorship. Open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); promising undergraduates welcome; no strict degree requirements. Relevant areas include public policy, political science, engineering, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement, and team fit. APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Application deadline not yet publicly stated; applications open June 2026 -- check programme page for the deadline.

Open call details

GovAI's UK Winter Fellowship (Research Track) is a three-month in-person fellowship in London for researchers and policy professionals seeking to enter or advance careers in AI governance and policy. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice, paired with a GovAI supervisor from the team or network; the first two weeks are spent exploring the AI governance landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Claude Corps is a national fellowship program from Anthropic placing early-career fellows full-time at nonprofits across America for one year, with the goal of extending AI's benefits to communities and building fellows' AI skills. Anthropic funds the program and provides Claude expertise; CodePath (America's largest collegiate CS education provider) is the fellows' official employer of record and leads programming; Social Finance leads measurement and evaluation. item.title: SOUND/IMAGE 2026: "Artistic Intelligence" - Call for Works
item.description:
Organisation: University of Greenwich

Location: University of Greenwich, London, UK; in-person attendance required.

Award: No payment to artists. All accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25 (covers catering and technical support).

Duration: Festival runs 5-8 November 2026.

Deadline: Closed 19 Jun 2026

SOUND/IMAGE 2026 is the 11th edition of the University of Greenwich festival exploring sound and image practice, running 5-8 November 2026 in London. This year's theme is "Artistic Intelligence": a critical reflection on creative choice, agency, and authenticity in the context of algorithmic and AI-mediated art making. The festival cites reference points from Vera Molnár, Pauline Oliveros, John Cage, Iannis Xenakis, Ryoji Ikeda, and Refik Anadol. Special consideration is given to students, apprentices, and emerging artists; submissions are welcome from composers, filmmakers, artists, performers, and architects. CATEGORIES ACCEPTED: sound and music works; audiovisual works; talks; artist statements; workshops and practical demonstrations; live performances; installations. THEMATIC AREAS: embodied knowledge and lived experience in aesthetic judgement; creative choice vs chance encounters; ownership, authenticity, and AI co-creation; human and algorithmic capability; spatial and immersive audio-visual forms; interdisciplinary practice (CreaTech); practice research. SUBMISSION RULES: works under 15 minutes in duration (installations exempt from duration limit); maximum three submissions per artist across all categories; artists must attend and present in person; submit separately for each submission via Microsoft CMT. FESTIVAL PASS: all accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25. Notifications before end of July 2026. Deadline: 19 June 2026, 23:59 UTC+1 (extended from 12 June). Submit at https://blogs.gre.ac.uk/sound-image/sound-image-2026-call-for-works/.

Open call details

item.title: Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World (Joint Funding Call)
item.description:
Organisation: Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, the Advanced Research & Invention Agency (ARIA), the Cooperative AI Foundation, and Google.org

Location: Global; open worldwide, with collaborations across geographic boundaries encouraged.

Award: Two funding tiers (applicants may apply to either or both), projects 1 to 2 years. Tier 1: up to USD 300,000 for exploratory research, pilot studies, or focused technical investigations. Tier 2: USD 300,000 to USD 1,000,000 for more ambitious or collaborative projects. For projects funded by Schmidt Sciences, indirect costs must be at or below 10%. No application fee.

Duration: 1 to 2 years

Deadline: 08 Aug 2026

A joint philanthropic funding call by Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, ARIA, the Cooperative AI Foundation and Google.org to catalyse the foundational scientific research needed to understand, evaluate and control risks emerging from large-scale ecosystems of interacting AI agents deployed by multiple actors (multi-principal, multi-agent settings). The call argues that focusing only on the safety and alignment of individual models is insufficient, and seeks system-level approaches. RESEARCH AGENDA (four clusters): (1) Sandboxes and Testbeds, realistic, reproducible multi-agent environments for studying populations of frontier-model agents; (2) The Science of Agent Networks, how collective capabilities emerge and scale, how networks fail, and how dangerous population-level properties can be detected; (3) Strengthening Agent Infrastructure, evaluating and stress-testing primitives such as identity, verifiability, reputation, communication and commitment; (4) Multi-Agent Oversight and Control, detection, attribution, security and intervention methods to keep deployed agent populations safe at scale. Proposals may target one cluster or span several, with priority on depth over breadth and on realistic sandboxes and testbeds. OUT OF SCOPE: single-agent safety, capability advancement without a clear safety motivation, toy systems, naive application of pre-existing solutions, commercial product development, and purely conceptual/policy work. ELIGIBILITY: individual researchers, research teams, research institutions and multi-institution collaborations across universities, national laboratories, institutes and nonprofit research organisations; open globally. Informational webinars on 30 June and 23 July 2026; notification of decisions in autumn 2026. Proposals due 8 August 2026 by 11:59pm Anywhere on Earth. Contact: multiagentsafety@schmidtsciences.org. Apply at https://schmidtsciences.smapply.io/prog/scaling_ai_safety_for_a_multi_agent_world/.

Open call details

item.title: Frame Fellowship 2.0 (Residential Accelerator for AI Safety Content Creators) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Frame Fellowship

Location: San Francisco, USA; residential (in person).

Award: USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks, plus accommodation, meals and flights covered. Access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, a mentor and creator network, and connections to funding, sponsorship and partnership opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic. Top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. No application fee.

Duration: 10-week residential program

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

Frame Fellowship is a 10-week residential accelerator in San Francisco for content creators educating the public on AI safety. Frame's premise is that content creators can solve a long-standing bottleneck of expanding reach and trust beyond the AI safety and effective altruism (EA) bubble; creators today hack attention, empower mass action and shape culture. Past Frame Fellows include award-winning filmmakers, journalists from leading newsrooms and ex-activists who transitioned to educating, empowering and preparing their communities for the impacts of AGI. Frame helps amplify their impact to millions of people, connects them to funding and sponsorship opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic, and helps establish their path as a leading content creator. This cohort runs 10 weeks with a stronger mentor and creator network, access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, housing, food and a stipend, plus connections to funding and partnership opportunities after the program. TWO TRACKS: (1) Independent Track, build your own channel and become a defining voice on a topic; (2) Amplifier Track, embed with an AI org/company and grow their reach. SUPPORT: Frame covers accommodation, meals, flights and a USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks so fellows can go all in; top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. Early deadline: 10 July 2026. Apply at https://framefellowship.com/.

item.title: Onassis ONX & ACE: Summer School 2026, Innovation, Tech & Culture (Worldbuilding)
item.description:
Organisation: Onassis ONX, in collaboration with ACE (Athens Center for Entrepreneurship & Innovation, AUEB) and NYU Tandon School of Engineering

Location: Athens, Greece (Athens University of Economics and Business); in-person attendance required.

Award: Free educational program (NO participation fee and no application fee), not a paid grant: there is no stipend, and travel and accommodation are not mentioned as covered. BENEFITS: lectures and mentorship from internationally recognised artists, designers, entrepreneurs and tech professionals (AUEB, NYU Tandon, and Onassis ONX Fellows in Athens and New York); access to funding opportunities and an incubation program for selected teams after completion; exposure to cutting-edge digital tools and interdisciplinary collaboration; and networking. Limited to 30 participants.

Duration: Two weeks, 1 to 13 July 2026

Deadline: Closed 21 Jun 2026

The Summer School: Innovation, Tech & Culture, organised by Onassis ONX and ACE for a third consecutive year, invites creative professionals and teams to explore the convergence of art, technology and sustainable entrepreneurship, bridging Culture and Digital Arts with Technology, Artificial Intelligence and Entrepreneurship. The 2026 edition is again dedicated to 'Worldbuilding', a methodology that fuels innovation and fosters novel entrepreneurial models: the emphasis shifts from the project or end product to the narratives that frame and sustain them, building immersive worlds around products and turning the audience into co-shapers of the experience. The program challenges traditional product design and promotion practices by harnessing new technologies, art thinking and innovative design. SYLLABUS: Media & Art History; Speculative & Interactive Design Theory; Ethics, Art & Technology; Computational Creativity; AI-enabled Tech Development; Innovation & Entrepreneurship (business modeling and product validation); building for speed with venture capital backing; understanding the market and audience; branding, marketing and digital channels; and IP management, licensing and contracts. Participants attend lectures and receive mentorship from keynote speakers of AUEB, the NYU Tandon School of Engineering and Onassis ONX Fellows, plus talks by leading international professionals such as artist-technologists Jiabao Li and Cooper Galvin. OPPORTUNITIES: access to funding opportunities and an incubation program for selected teams after completion; development of entrepreneurial, artistic and technological skills; and networking and growth opportunities for participating companies. WHO CAN APPLY: undergraduate and postgraduate students and early-career professionals; established professionals and corporate employees in arts, culture, design, marketing, technology and business administration. PARTICIPATION: individual application, or as a member of a corporate team or company representative; in-person attendance required; working language English; up to 30 participants. NOTE: this is a free educational program rather than a paid grant; no participation or application fee. Apply via the Onassis Directory by 21 June 2026. Inquiries: digital@onassis.org.

item.title: Prototype Fund Switzerland 2026-27: Responsible and Sustainable AI
item.description:
Organisation: Prototype Fund Switzerland (Opendata.ch)

Location: Switzerland; applicants must be eligible to work in Switzerland.

Award: Up to CHF 50,000 per project, plus coaching, mentoring and workshops and access to a tech/policy/society network. No application fee.

Duration: 4-month prototyping phase (mid-October 2026 to mid-February 2027)

Deadline: 07 Sep 2026

Since 2020 the Prototype Fund Switzerland has strengthened the common good with open-source, public-interest technology, supporting interdisciplinary teams to develop and test early-stage projects that show how open source, data and digital technologies such as AI can create real societal value. The 2026-2027 edition focuses on Responsible and Sustainable AI: building the path toward a more responsible and sustainable AI, tech and digitalization landscape, open source and beyond. THIS YEAR'S FOCUS: projects that reduce resource use, energy consumption or emissions; strengthen accountability, transparency and governance; and contribute to resilient and trustworthy digital systems, including sustainable digitalization and digital sufficiency, public-interest AI and data infrastructures, and tools for governance, oversight or digital sovereignty. PUBLIC INTEREST TECH areas span sustainability and climate, participation and civic tech, education and literacy, health and inclusion, and open knowledge and data. WHO THEY FUND: individuals and small teams open to open source, AI and iterative experimentation, eager to combine technical capability with societal and political awareness, willing to share learnings, motivated to work on pressing societal problems through technology, with an early-stage but well-thought-out concept, and eligible to work in Switzerland. SUPPORT: up to CHF 50,000 in funding, a 4-month prototyping phase, coaching, mentoring and workshops, and access to a strong network across tech, policy and society. Teams are strongly encouraged (though not required) to publish code, data or components under open-source licenses and to document their approach. TIMELINE: applications 21 July to 7 September 2026; rolling jury selection August-September 2026; prototyping mid-October 2026 to mid-February 2027; outputs and learnings November 2026 to July 2027. Questions: info@prototypefund.ch.

Open call details

A 12-week fall fellowship that bridges emerging AI technology and the social sector: fellows gain practical, workplace-ready AI skills through hands-on, interactive training while applying them to increase the operational capacity of a partner nonprofit. The program emphasises moving past theoretical AI concepts into immediate, real-world applications, and provides equitable access to AI training for individuals and organizations driving positive social change. WHO SHOULD APPLY: professionals and emerging leaders seeking practical AI skills; prior AI experience is not required. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be at least 18, secure a commitment from a 501(c)(3) nonprofit partner, and commit roughly 8-10 hours per week. item.description:

Fast Grants fund high-ambition, curiosity-driven pilot projects at the intersection of AI and health, for early-career scientists or established researchers pivoting into the field. THEMES: training, fine-tuning or evaluating models on biomedical data; exploring or assembling datasets that could unlock new clinical or biological insight; building AI tools or agents that scale a scientist's leverage; pilot experiments that de-risk a bigger funding ask; and anything else where modest, fast capital meaningfully accelerates an AI- or data-driven path to a health breakthrough. item.title: ZwillGen Fellowship Program 2027-2028 (Technology Law)
item.description:
Organisation: ZwillGen

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (must work out of the DC office)

Award: Salary USD 115,000 to USD 125,000, with a reduced annual billable target (1,250 hours) to create space for writing, professional development, and policy engagement. No application fee.

Duration: 12-month position beginning Fall 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The ZwillGen Fellowship is a 12-month position for recent law-school graduates and early-career attorneys interested in working at the intersection of law, technology, and public policy. Fellows work alongside attorneys on matters involving privacy, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, online platforms, surveillance, digital advertising, fintech, online safety, and technology regulation, contributing to client counseling, investigations, litigation, and emerging regulatory matters, as well as research, writing, and speaking. The program includes a reduced billable target (1,250 hours) to make room for thought leadership and engagement with the technology policy, privacy, and civil liberties community. ELIGIBILITY: must have graduated from law school before the program starts; must have taken or plan to take a bar exam near the start of the Fellowship; must be able to work out of the DC office and be eligible to apply for admission to the DC Bar. Candidates primarily interested in traditional IP practice may find it less aligned. A small number of applicants may also be considered for the Hannah Schaller Memorial Skylark Fellowship (privacy-law focus, with a reduced 1,100-hour billable target for additional community engagement). Following the Fellowship, fellows may be invited into a full-time attorney role or supported in pursuing other technology law and policy opportunities. APPLY with a resume, law-school transcript, writing sample, and cover letter by 31 August 2026.

item.title: Principles of Intelligence: Research Scientist (Ambitious Mechanistic Interpretability)
item.description:
Organisation: Principles of Intelligence (PrincInt)

Location: Fully remote (strong preference for candidates in or able to spend time in London, UK and/or the Bay Area, US)

Award: Estimated salary USD 100,000 to USD 250,000 gross, depending on location and experience. Benefits include 4 weeks paid holiday plus additional time off and flexible hours. Visa sponsorship possible for candidates based in or relocating to London (UK) or Berkeley/Bay Area (US). No application fee.

Duration: Full-time (or contractor); start date July 2026

Deadline: Closed 21 Jun 2026

Principles of Intelligence (PrincInt) is hiring Research Scientists to work on Ambitious Mechanistic Interpretability (AMI): developing tractable, realistic, scale-aware data structure models; quantifying the relationship between data structure and the features AI systems learn; and building synthetic datasets to benchmark and improve interpretability tools. The team is currently exploring a data model based on high-dimensional percolation theory (a statistically self-similar, sparse structure), working at the intersection of physics and mechanistic interpretability. The role is FULLY REMOTE and async. MUST HAVE: a PhD or equivalent research experience in a technical field (math, physics, neuroscience, computer science, or complexity science); ability to communicate complex novel ideas; working knowledge of neural networks and deep learning; experience with scientific computing in Python; comfort in a fully remote environment; and strong motivation to contribute to AI safety. GREAT TO HAVE: a publication record in ML or a quantitative field; knowledge of statistical physics (percolation theory, renormalization), mechanistic interpretability, stochastic/coalescent processes, combinatorics, or network science; experience with fractal geometry, neural scaling laws, graph/tree algorithms, or theories of concepts in cognitive science; or familiarity with the ARENA curriculum. The team meets in person 2-4 times a year. PROCESS: application, screening call, remote interview, research talk, a paid one-day remote work trial, and references. Apply by 21 June 2026; applications reviewed on a rolling basis. Accommodations: info@princint.ai.

Open call details

item.title: Cambridge AI Safety Hub: Hardware Assurance Programme 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Cambridge AI Safety Hub (CAISH)

Location: Cambridge, UK (in-person, at Meridian)

Award: GBP 1,500 stipend, plus travel, accommodation and food covered. No application fee.

Duration: Six days, 17-22 August 2026

Deadline: Closed 07 Jun 2026

A six-day intensive in Cambridge to build the mechanisms that let third parties verify what AI chips are computing, where they are operating, and at what scale, while preserving confidentiality. Hardware assurance covers tamper-evident enclosures, telemetry and network monitoring, attestation protocols, and cryptographic approaches to inference verification, technical infrastructure that AI governance increasingly depends on but that does not yet exist. The cohort of twenty spends the week in talks, working sessions and project work with domain-expert residents (from the Future of Life Institute, MIRI, Longview Philanthropy and others), presenting their work on Day 6. WHO SHOULD APPLY: practising engineers, researchers, PhD students, and technically strong undergraduate or master's students with depth in one of: silicon and firmware (RTL, ASIC, SoC, FPGA, embedded); hardware security (side-channel countermeasures, tamper protection); cryptography and formal methods (applied crypto, zero-knowledge proofs, formal verification); trusted execution (TEEs, attestation, secure boot, root-of-trust); ML systems (distributed training with NCCL/Megatron/DeepSpeed, large-scale inference); or networking (line-rate packet processing, deep packet inspection). Years of industry experience are not required; strong technical projects, research or coursework can be enough. Example open problems include tamper-evident enclosures for liquid-cooled AI accelerators, mutually trusted attestation hardware for international agreements, inference verification at scale, and workload classification from hardware telemetry. Day 6 also covers next steps including Coefficient Giving's Career Development and Transition Funding and BlueDot Impact's Rapid Grants (up to USD 10,000). APPLY by 7 June 2026, 23:59 Anywhere on Earth.

Open call details

item.title: CHAI: Research Fellowship (Center for Human-Compatible AI, UC Berkeley)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI), UC Berkeley

Location: Berkeley, California, USA (in-person)

Award: Salary commensurate with qualifications and experience, on UC Berkeley salary scales (negotiated). Funding for two years, renewable up to three given satisfactory performance. Eligible for visa sponsorship, with CHAI also contributing to visa costs for fellows and their dependents. No application fee.

Duration: Two years, renewable up to three

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The CHAI Research Fellowship trains highly qualified postdoctoral researchers to advance beneficial AI, working with CHAI faculty Stuart Russell, Pieter Abbeel and Anca Dragan and collaborating with affiliate faculty at Berkeley AI Research and beyond. Fellows have broad freedom to pursue novel research toward provably beneficial AI systems in areas such as reasoning, decision making, learning, multi-agent systems and philosophical foundations, using probability theory, game theory and control theory. Current topics of interest include theoretical foundations of intelligent agent architecture, expressive formal languages for probability models (including probabilistic programming), decision making over long time scales, cumulative lifelong learning, human-in-the-loop planning and reinforcement learning, human-robot interaction, the structure of human preferences, acting on behalf of multiple humans, robust cooperation in heterogeneous multi-agent systems, and mechanism design for human-machine systems. Fellows may also lead collaborations, advise junior researchers, and teach. QUALIFICATIONS: a PhD (or about to be obtained) in a relevant technical discipline (computer science, statistics, mathematics, or theoretical economics) and a record of high-quality published research; prior work on the AI control problem is not required. International applicants are eligible (visa sponsorship provided). TO APPLY: submit via the CHAI application form a resume, a one-page statement of interest describing the research you would like to undertake, and the names and emails of two academic referees. Start date is flexible and applications are reviewed on a rolling basis (no fixed deadline). General enquiries: chai-info@berkeley.edu.

Open call details
